excerpt: Defines a tree of keypairs derived from a single seed via parent-to-child derivation. The motivation includes giving wallets the ability to generate a fresh receiving address for every payment without having to back up new key material, which makes default-non-reuse the path of least resistance.
body:
Source excerpt:
Defines a tree of keypairs derived from a single seed via parent-to-child derivation. The motivation includes giving wallets the ability to generate a fresh receiving address for every payment without having to back up new key material, which makes default-non-reuse the path of least resistance.

---

Cited by leaf "Address reuse and the default privacy floor":
If two payments arrive at the same Bitcoin address, anyone watching the chain can permanently link them — same recipient, same balance, same spending history once those coins move. There is no cryptographic step required; it's just public data joined on a string match. This is the single biggest privacy leak in everyday Bitcoin use, and it's the one the protocol gives you the most help avoiding [1].

HD wallets (BIP32) were designed around this. From a single seed, a wallet derives a fresh receiving address for every incoming payment along a deterministic path, so the user never has to manage keys by hand or copy old addresses [2]. Modern wallets default to this — you tap "receive" and get a new address each time. The leak shows up when people paste a static address into a bio, a donation page, or a shop checkout, or when an exchange withdrawal gets reused as an inbound for the next deposit.

The other half of the same problem is the common-input-ownership heuristic: when a transaction spends multiple inputs, the surveillance assumption is that one wallet controls all of them [3]. Combine that with one reused address and a chain analyst can usually walk a substantial portion of someone's UTXO set in a few hops. Coinjoin, payjoin, and silent payments all exist to break some piece of this default linkage; the cheapest defense, however, is just not reusing addresses in the first place.

---

Cited by leaf "HD wallets and BIP32 derivation":
An HD (hierarchical deterministic) wallet derives every one of its keys from a single seed via a deterministic tree. From the seed you compute a master extended key; from that master, child keys at any path you like; from any child, more children, indefinitely [1]. The practical consequence: backing up the seed backs up the entire wallet — current keys, future keys, every account you'll ever derive from it — without needing to copy out individual private keys ever.

The derivation path you see on wallet screens — usually written `m/84'/0'/0'/0/5` or similar — encodes which branch of the tree the key lives on. The BIP44/49/84/86 conventions assign meaning to the first few levels: purpose (script type — 84' is native segwit, 86' is taproot), coin type (0' is Bitcoin), account index, change-or-receive, and address index [2]. Two wallets that agree on those conventions can both sweep the same seed and find the same coins; that's why "recover seed in any BIP39-compatible wallet" works in practice.

The `'` mark means the derivation step is hardened: the parent's private key is required to derive the child. Without hardening, the math allows a third party with the parent xpub plus any child private key to compute the parent private key — fine inside a single wallet that controls everything, catastrophic if you've shared an xpub for watch-only purposes [3]. This is why all account-level paths are hardened: it lets you safely hand out an account xpub for receive-address generation by another device without exposing the master.
